question: Describe the purpose of the 'd' parameter in the ARIMA(p,d,q) notation and explain how one determines its appropriate integer value.
expectedPoints: The 'd' parameter represents the order of differencing required to make a non-stationary time series stationary.
It is determined by applying successive differences until the resulting series passes statistical tests for stationarity (e.g., ADF test).
The value 'd' is the minimum number of differences needed to achieve stationarity.

question: Explain how an ARMA(p,q) model differs fundamentally from an ARIMA(p,d,q) model. What critical assumption does the ARMA model make that the ARIMA model is designed to handle?
expectedPoints: An ARMA model assumes the underlying time series is stationary.
An ARIMA model incorporates an initial differencing step (the 'I' for Integrated) to transform a non-stationary series into a stationary one.
Therefore, an ARMA(p,q) model is a special case of an ARIMA(p,0,q) model, applied only after stationarity has been achieved.

question: Outline the complete step-by-step procedure for building an ARIMA model, from initial data preparation to final model diagnostic checking. Justify the purpose of each key step in the process.
expectedPoints: Step 1: Visual Inspection & Stationarity Testing: Plot the data and use tests (e.g., ADF) to determine if differencing ('d') is needed.
Step 2: Model Identification: Examine ACF/PACF plots of the stationary data to identify potential 'p' (AR) and 'q' (MA) orders.
Step 3: Parameter Estimation: Use maximum likelihood estimation to fit the ARIMA(p,d,q) model and obtain coefficients.
Step 4: Diagnostic Checking: Analyze residuals to ensure they resemble white noise (no autocorrelation, constant variance); if not, return to Step 2.
Step 5: Forecasting: Use the validated model to generate forecasts and prediction intervals.
